core_claim: GreenPlasma is a full system-level privilege escalation that exploits a trusted Windows process. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as part of the June 2026 vulnerability wave. Patched in June 2026 Patch Tuesday.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released GreenPlasma (CVE-2026-45586) as the fifth exploit in their June 2026 wave. A full system-level privilege escalation that exploits a trusted Windows process running as SYSTEM.

The mechanism abuses a DLL search order hijack in a SYSTEM-scheduled task. The task runs a trusted Microsoft-signed binary that loads a DLL from a user-writable path. The researcher places a malicious DLL. The task triggers. SYSTEM loads it. Full system compromise.

Works on fully patched Windows 10 and 11 before June 2026 Patch Tuesday. Deterministic SYSTEM shell. No race. No brute force. The binary is signed by Microsoft. The path is writable.

The scheduled task is Microsoft-signed. The binary is Microsoft-signed. The DLL path is the only anomaly. EDRs allow signed binaries. The writable path is a configuration issue. No exploit binary on disk — just a DLL in a user profile.

Deploy June 2026 Patch Tuesday. Audit scheduled tasks for user-writable DLL paths. Enable DLL blocking rules (AppLocker/WDAC). Monitor for signed binaries loading from non-system paths.

The patch fixes the path. But the pattern — signed binary, writable path, SYSTEM context — recurs. Defenders must hunt the pattern, not just the CVE.

A Microsoft-signed binary is not a trust boundary if its load path is writable. The signature validates the author. The path validates the runtime.

Do you audit scheduled task DLL paths for writability?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[GreenPlasma, CVE-2026-45586, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, system LPE, DLL hijack, scheduled task, SYSTEM process, AppLocker, WDAC, signed binary abuse]
comment_prompt: Do you audit scheduled task DLL paths for writability?
alt_text: Title card: GreenPlasma hijacks a trusted Windows process for full SYSTEM access. CVE-2026-45586 is a system-level privilege escalation via trusted process abuse, Nightmare Eclipse exploit five, patched June 2026. A green plasma tendril wraps around a process icon and turns it into a SYSTEM token.

GreenPlasma was the fifth exploit Nightmare Eclipse released. CVE-2026-45586 gained full SYSTEM access through a trusted Windows process. Patched June 2026 Patch Tuesday, but it was already public before the fix. A glowing process sphere lifts toward a crown of light.

The target process loads attacker code with no memory corruption. A trusted Windows system process running as SYSTEM legitimately invokes a library from a user-writable path, giving attackers a clean, deterministic escalation on Windows 10 and 11 before June 2026. A green tendril threads into a glowing binary orb without breaking its surface.

A SYSTEM-scheduled task loads a library from a user-writable path. A Microsoft-signed binary runs as part of that task and Windows resolves the library from a location any local user can write to. A green tendril curves from a bright core toward an outer ring of dim open space.

Both the task and the binary carry Microsoft signatures. The only anomaly is the library load path. Endpoint detection allows signed binaries through, so the writable location goes undetected. Two glowing orbs with green verification halos and a thin red thread rising between them.

The patch fixes this path, but the underlying pattern keeps recurring. Audit scheduled tasks for user-writable library load paths, enable AppLocker or WDAC blocking rules, and monitor signed binaries loading from outside the system directory. Two process orbs, one with a full shield ring and one with a gap in its ring.

A signed binary is not a trust boundary when its load path is writable. The signature proves authorship. The path proves runtime integrity. A signed binary icon split between a green certificate glow and a green library tendril entering from a writable directory.
Closing question: Do you audit your scheduled task library load paths for writability? A single glowing process orb sits on a clean dark end-card with a question-mark shimmer above it.

narration.text: GreenPlasma is a privilege escalation exploit that turns a trusted Windows process into a full SYSTEM shell.
It was the fifth vulnerability Nightmare Eclipse released in June 2026, tracked as CVE-2026-45586, and it was already public when Microsoft patched it on Patch Tuesday.
The trick is a library search-order hijack inside a SYSTEM-scheduled task, where a Microsoft-signed binary loads a file from a path any local user can write to.

The attacker drops a crafted library into that path, the task fires on schedule, and Windows hands over a SYSTEM shell with no race condition, no brute force, and nothing on disk except one file in a user profile.
Defenders miss it because both the task and the binary carry Microsoft signatures, so endpoint tools wave them through, and only the writable path reveals the anomaly.
The fix is the June 2026 Patch Tuesday update, but you should also audit every scheduled task for user-writable library load paths and enable AppLocker or WDAC blocking rules.
The key lesson is that a code signature tells you who wrote the binary, not whether its runtime environment is safe to run in.
Do you audit your scheduled task library load paths? Drop a yes or no in the comments.

beats.caption: GreenPlasma hijacks trusted process. Full SYSTEM.
Patched June. Public before patch. Core SYSTEM component.
DLL hijack in SYSTEM task. Signed binary. User path.
Deterministic. No race. DLL in user profile. SYSTEM loads.
Both signed. Path is anomaly. EDRs allow signed.
Patch. Audit paths. DLL rules. Hunt signed + user path.
Signature validates author. Path validates runtime.
Do you audit task DLL paths?
